chunks.page_summary: This article provides instructions on how to configure Ignition SCADA to visualize Rheonics sensor data transmitted via an MQTT broker, specifically using HiveMQ.
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863

Using Ignition SCADA to Visualize Rheonics Sensor Data via MQTT

Modified on: Mon, 29 Sep, 2025 at 10:39 PM

What is the purpose of this article?
This article outlines the steps to configure Ignition SCADA by Inductive Automation® so it can connect to an MQTT broker (HiveMQ in this guide) and visualize Rheonics sensor data.

TABLE OF CONTENTS
  1. Configuring Ignition SCADA
    1.1. Install MQTT Modules
    1.2. Configure MQTT Distributor with HiveMQ
    1.3. Configure MQTT Engine with HiveMQ
  2. Ignition Designer
  3. Resources

1. Configuring Ignition SCADA
Using the Ignition SCADA software, it is possible to connect to an MQTT broker and subscribe to the topics where Rheonics sensor data is published. Once the data is received, the Ignition Designer software can be used to build an application based on the Rheonics template, as shown in the figure.

Figure 1: Visualization of sensor parameter through Ignition Designer

To learn how to send Rheonics sensor data to an MQTT broker using Node-RED refer to Integrating Rheonics Sensors with Node-RED and MQTT for Remote Data Access.

1.1. Install MQTT Modules
1. Download and install the following MQTT modules from Inductive Automation (Strategic Partner Modules (https://inductiveautomation.com/downloads/third-party-modules/8.1.7)):
  * MQTT Engine
  * MQTT Transmission
  * MQTT Distributor

MQTT Distributor Module (36.3 MB) 4.0.29 sha-256
MQTT Engine Module (47.4 MB) 4.0.29 sha-256
MQTT Transmission Module (33.8 MB) 4.0.29 sha-256
Figure 2: MQTT modules

This guide applies for MQTT Engine 4.0.10 or later installed.

2. In Ignition, go to Config>>Modules.
key_facts: This article outlines steps to configure Ignition SCADA by Inductive Automation to connect to an MQTT broker and visualize Rheonics sensor data.
Ignition SCADA software can connect to an MQTT broker and subscribe to topics where Rheonics sensor data is published.
Ignition Designer software can be used to build an application based on a Rheonics template.
Rheonics sensor data can be sent to an MQTT broker using Node-RED.
The required MQTT modules from Inductive Automation include MQTT Engine, MQTT Transmission, and MQTT Distributor.
The listed MQTT modules (Distributor, Engine, Transmission) are available in version 4.0.29.
This guide is applicable for MQTT Engine version 4.0.10 or later installations.
warnings_notes: This guide applies for MQTT Engine 4.0.10 or later installed.

1.2. Configure MQTT Distributor with HiveMQ
To learn how to create and configure the HiveMQ broker refer to HiveMQ Broker Integration with Rheonics (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/8100041868)
1. Go to the MQTT Distributor Settings located in the Config menu.
Figure 7: MQTT Distributor Settings
2. In the General tab, put the following configuration:

MQTT modules are installed by selecting a .modl file.
After installation, three new MQTT modules (Distributor, Engine, Transmission) are expected to appear.
The document refers to a Rheonics support page for configuring the HiveMQ broker.
MQTT Distributor settings are accessed via the 'Config' menu in Ignition.

1.3. Configure MQTT Engine with HiveMQ
1. Go to the MQTT Engine Settings located on the Config menu.
key_facts: The MQTT Server can be enabled or disabled via a checkbox in the Main section.
Plain TCP connections for the MQTT Server can be enabled or disabled, defaulting to port 8883.
Websocket connections for the MQTT Server can be enabled or disabled, defaulting to port 8.
Enabling TLS for the MQTT Server requires a TLS certificate to be uploaded in Ignition.
The TLS enabled MQTT Server operates on port 8883.
Secure Websocket connections for the MQTT Server can be enabled or disabled, defaulting to port 9443.
New MQTT users are created from the 'Users' tab by clicking 'Create new MQTT Users'.
MQTT user credentials (username and password) should match those created in the HiveMQ broker.
User permissions (ACLs) for MQTT are specified as 'RW#' for reading and writing permission.
Changes to MQTT user configurations are saved using the 'Save Changes' button.
Successfully created users are listed under the 'Users' tab with options to delete or edit.
The MQTT Engine settings are located in the 'Config' menu for further configuration with HiveMQ.
diagram_labels: Enable plain TCP connections for the MQTT Server (Checkbox Description)
Enable Websocket connections for the MQTT Server (Checkbox Description)
Non-TLS MQTT Server Websocket port (Field Description)

Note: For additional details on configuring MQTT Distributor, see the documentation here (Note Text)

5. In the Settings tab, add the Server information.

• Password: As configured in HiveMQ and the MQTT Distributor
key_facts: To access MQTT Engine settings, navigate through the Config menu.
MQTT Server Sets are configured within the 'Sets' tab.
A new MQTT Server Set is initiated by clicking 'Create new MQTT Server Set'.
When creating an MQTT Server Set, a unique name must be provided.
The 'Primary Host Enabled' option should be unselected for the new MQTT Server Set.
If 'Primary Host Enabled' is true, the 'Primary Host ID' field must also be set.
The 'Primary Host ID' field is optional and allows connecting clients to remain connected to the application.
MQTT Server Settings are configured in the 'Servers' tab.
A new MQTT Server Setting is initiated by clicking 'Create new MQTT Server Setting'.
Server information, including Name, URL, Username, and Password, must be added in the 'Settings' tab.
The server's Name can be 'HiveMQ' or any other descriptive name.
The URL for the MQTT server requires the 'ssl:' prefix and the TLS MQTT URL found in HiveMQ Connection details.
The Username and Password for the MQTT server must be identical to those configured in HiveMQ and the MQTT Distributor.
diagram_labels: The friendly name of this MQTT Server Set
Description of this MQTT Server Set
Whether or not primary host STATE message will be published. If true, the Primary Host ID field should also be set
The Primary Host ID to allow connecting clients to ensure they remain connected to this application (optional)
warnings_notes: For additional details on configuring MQTT Engine, see the documentation here.
Whether or not primary host STATE message will be published. If true, the Primary Host ID field should also be set.
The Primary Host ID to allow connecting clients to ensure they remain connected to this application (optional).
The URL should be added with 'ssl:' prefix followed by the 'TLS MQTT URL' found in the HiveMQ Connection details.
The Username must be the same as configured in HiveMQ and the MQTT Distributor.
The Password must be the same as configured in HiveMQ and the MQTT Distributor.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides step-by-step instructions for configuring MQTT Engine server and namespace settings, including defining custom namespaces, subscriptions for sensor data, and tag folder organization within Ignition.
chunks.extracted_text:
6. Next, go to the Namespaces tab and click on Create new Custom Namespace.

Figure 16: MQTT Engine Server Configuration
  Tabs: General, Servers, Sets, Namespaces
  Servers Sub-tabs: Settings, Certificates
  Main Section:
    Name: HiveMQ (The friendly name of this MQTT Server Setting)
    Enabled: [checkbox checked] Enable this MQTT Server Setting
    URL: ssl://07b0d1199eb84b88bc6f97944f (The URL of the MQTT Server to connect to. Should be of the form tcp://mydomain.com:1883 or ssl://mydomain.com:8883)
    Server Set: HiveMQ (The Server Set this MQTT Server is associated with)
    Username: rheonics (The username for connections if required by the MQTT Server (optional))
    Change Password?: [checkbox unchecked] Check this box to change the existing password.
    Password: [masked] (The password for connections if required by the MQTT Server (optional))
    Password: [masked] (Re-type password for verification.)

Figure 17: Namespace Creation in MQTT Engine
  Tabs: General, Servers, Sets, Namespaces (Namespaces tab highlighted)
  Namespace Sub-tabs: Default, Custom
  Table Columns: Name, Subscriptions, Root Tag Folder, Tag Name, JSON Payload
  Button: Create new Custom Namespace...

7. Configure as follows in the Custom>>General Tab:
  Main
    Name: Name of the custom namespace (Example: test)
    Subscriptions: In this section, subscribe to the topics where your sensor data is published. If there is more than one topic, write it like topic1,topic2,... separated by commas. In this case, if there are two different topics, one for viscosity and one for temperature, the names of the topics would be srv/temperature,srv/viscosity
  Optional
    Root Tag Folder: Name of the folder that will appear in Ignition with all the tags of the topics (Example: test).

Figure 18: Namespace Configuration in MQTT Engine
  Tabs: General, Sets, String Conversion (General tab highlighted)
  Main Section:
    Name: test (The name of this custom namespace)
    Subscriptions: srv/viscosity,srv/temperature (Comma separated list of topics the the MQTT server will subscribe on)
  Optional Section:
    Root Tag Folder: test (The root folder where all tags will be located (optional))
    Tag Name: [empty] (The name of the tag. If left blank the last token in the topic will represent the tag (optional))
    JSON Payload: [checkbox unchecked] Parse the payload as a JSON string (optional)
    Encoding Charset: UTF-8 (The encoding format to use for Strings when not parsing as JSON)
    Show advanced properties: [checkbox unchecked]
  Button: Create New Custom Namespace

8. In Sets, ensure that only the set you previously created remains. If there are any others that don't correspond, delete them.

Figure 19: Sets in Namespace Configuration
  Tabs: General, Servers, Sets, Namespaces
  Namespace Sub-tabs: Default, Custom
  Configuration Sub-tabs: General, Sets, String Conversion (Sets tab highlighted)
  Server Set:
    HiveMQ [delete] [edit]
  Button: Create new Namespace Server Set...

9. After saving the changes, a custom Namespace is created.
key_facts: The document provides instructions for configuring MQTT Engine server and namespace settings.
Multiple MQTT topics for sensor data, such as viscosity and temperature, can be subscribed to by separating them with commas.
A custom 'Root Tag Folder' can be defined to organize tags within Ignition.
The encoding format for strings when not parsing as JSON is UTF-8.
In the 'Sets' configuration, it is important to ensure only relevant server sets remain and to delete any that do not correspond to the setup.
warnings_notes: Note: This is a whitelist of server sets for custom namespace subscriptions. For additional details on configuring MQTT Engine, see the documentation here

2. Ignition Designer
1. Open the Ignition Designer app, and click on New Project.
Figure 22: New Ignition Designer Project

2. Next, put the Project information.
Figure 23: Project Information

3. Go to the Ignition Exchange portal (https://inductiveautomation.com/exchange/).
key_facts: A namespace with the name 'test' and subscription 'srv/viscosity.srv/temperature' has been created.
The HiveMQ server is connected with the username 'rheonics' and URL 'ssl://07b0d1199eb646b86c66197944f38036.s1.hivemq.cloud:8883'.
The Chariot SCADA server with URL 'tcp://localhost:1883' is not connected.
Outbound node and device tag writes are blocked by default in Ignition's MQTT Engine.
Creating a new project in Ignition Designer involves defining Project Settings and Connection Settings.
warnings_notes: Note: Outbound node and device tag writes are BLOCKED (see Advanced Settings tab) For additional details on configuring MQTT Engine, see the documentation here.

key_facts: The document provides step-by-step instructions for configuring a software interface to display Rheonics sensor data.
Users need to navigate to 'Templates>>Rheonics_SRV' in the Project Browser to access the relevant template.
The software interface displays real-time viscosity and temperature readings from a 'Viscometer SRV' unit, denoted as 'SRV 1'.
Display elements, such as an LED Display for viscosity, are linked to sensor data by modifying their 'Value' property in the Vision Property Editor.
Tag linking involves searching within 'All Providers>>MQTT Engine>>Root Tag Folder' to locate specific sensor data parameters, for instance, 'viscosity.value' under a 'test/srv' path.
The tag linking configuration allows for options like 'Bidirectional' data binding, setting a 'Fallback Delay' (shown as 2.5), and an 'Overlay Opt-Out'.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides instructions for visualizing Rheonics sensor data, specifically viscosity and temperature, within an Ignition SCADA system using LED displays, and lists various resources for integrating Rheonics sensors with MQTT, Node-RED, and other platforms.

key_facts: The page demonstrates the final visualization of sensor data from a Rheonics SME in an Ignition Designer window, specifically displaying viscosity and temperature.
Users are instructed to follow a similar procedure for configuring other LED displays to show desired parameters.
A direct link is provided for learning how to send multiple Rheonics sensor data via MQTT to Ignition SCADA.
The document lists several resources for integrating Rheonics SME with Node-RED and MQTT for remote data access.
Additional resources cover strategic partner modules, HiveMQ Broker integration with Rheonics, and the Ignition Exchange portal.
diagram_labels: Figure 37: Final Visualization of Sensor Data
